items.q: How are most microplastics formed in the environment?
items.correct: Larger plastic items break down physically into smaller fragments over time.
items.misconception: They are all manufactured at microscopic size deliberately.
items.why: Students think microplastics are designed small. Most are SECONDARY - formed when sun, waves, and wear break down larger items. Primary microplastics (microbeads, fibres) are a smaller proportion.

items.q: How does the Brundtland Report define sustainable development?
items.correct: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as 'no growth'. The Brundtland definition explicitly balances development AND environmental concerns - it's about INTERGENERATIONAL equity, not freezing change.

items.q: What is the difference between species richness and species evenness?
items.correct: Richness counts how many species exist; evenness measures how balanced their abundances are.
items.random_wrong: Richness is for plants, evenness for animals.
items.why: Students conflate these. A community with 10 species each having 100 individuals has high evenness; one with 10 species but 991 of species A and 1 of each other has low evenness despite identical richness.

items.q: Which cycle has no significant atmospheric phase?
items.why: Correct answer: Phosphorus. “Carbon” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Average time in a reservoir is called?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Residence time. “Flux” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximate residence time of CO2 in the atmosphere in years (centennial estimate)?
items.why: Exact answer: 100 years. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
